speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] of November 1995. You were telling me how you came to Australia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and-- [CLEARS THROAT] That's a story in itself. What happened was when I took over the camp, the first happening was the birth date of Queen Juliana-- at that time, Princess Juliana. That was on the 30th of April.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we arranged sort of a party in the camp. And I took the leading of it. And I took also the congratulations, for which we telegraphed through later on to Princess Juliana. And that was quite nice evening. I got the presents. We got the-- they made them out of [INAUDIBLE], you say that? That, that bread-- they made that. And in that, they made-- put a J and a B-- Juliana and Bernard, and some other things. So it was a nice evening. But the next day, it was the 1st of May, and that is Labor Day. And when I walked from my house into the camp, I said, hey, what's on here? And we had a big chimney stack, the highest point around. And everybody could see that around. And on top of it, it was the Communist flag. I said, what's on here? Huh? One day, it's orange, and the next it's red? Not for me. Get out that flag. But they did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was in my office and another gave me-- anyhow, the message that the flag was, was in the chimney again. So I went out, said, and now get that one out. And if you don't, I'll burn it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I collected the flag. And that's why one of the-- also a German, but he was a Communist. I knew that-- he came to me. He said, Mr. van As, you did the wrong thing. Was he Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, yeah. [? Blut. ?] [? Blut ?] was his name. No, a nice fellow in himself, but he was a communist. OK. That was his, his belief. OK. But the whole story came in the newspaper, in the communist newspaper, what they call the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. And in it, they gave me a threat that if the Communist-- I had done a bad thing towards an ally. And if he, the Communism, could control-- something in these words-- could control Holland [INAUDIBLE] states, then I was the first one that was going to be arrested. there's a full article in the newspaper. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I thought, well, you are warned. Be careful. So then in 1948, the Battle of Berlin started. I thought, well, now it becomes time to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: You never know how quickly the Russians could move over. The German did this in five days. The Russians could do it even quicker than that. So that's when I applied for America. And I got my quota number. But it did-- that took long and long. And it was never ending. So in the meantime, I applied also for a quota number in Canada. And then one day in July '55, a chap from the organization where I booked myself in, he came to our place. He was just-- had just been in Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, why aren't you going to Australia? I said, why should I go to Australia? You can't take any money with you. You can't take nothing. And I want to take something to care for the family, that we are not empty handed here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, well, things have changed. You can take now some money as well. I said, OK, if that's the case, book me in. And he said, well, then I make sure that you will be in Australia within three months time. And that happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the 28th of November. We left on the 25th of October.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who sent the letter to [INAUDIBLE]? Which of the people you saved sent the letter to [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah, in that time, we were back in Holland because I got sick. And that was a consequence of the camp, actually. My, my-- I had ulcers, four ulcers. Two peptic and two duodenal ones. And they are-- they were dormant to [INAUDIBLE]. But when we went over for holiday to Holland, that's where it got worse. And they wouldn't allow me to go back and let me in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's why I thought, well, I'm not going to survive this anymore. It got worse and worse. I thought, well, better someone get hold of my papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's where I contacted the Jewish consulate in The Hague, and said, I've got quite some papers here which I believe is very important to you. He said, show me. And when he saw it, he said, yes. They are really of importance to us. We will take care of it. So that was the start of how it came to [INAUDIBLE]. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you receive your medal? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: In '92, eh? Yeah, in '92. '92. It's on the certificate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you kept in contact with any of the people that you saved?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I'm still in contact with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: With who?

speaker: interviewee
text: There are two where I've got regular correspondence with.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are Hannah Gudema Meyer and Mimi, Mimi Cohen. Mimi Cohen, yeah. What was her husband's name? Ah, hang on. [? Couver-- ?] [? Vancouver. ?] [? Vancouver ?] Cohen.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you save them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kept them. They stayed there until the end.

speaker: interviewer
text: Working for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nope. There-- I had-- at the last moment-- that was in September '44-- I had to make my choice between the two of them. And that gave some fiction for these people in the first instance [INAUDIBLE]. Both of them were a bit-- yeah, reluctant to move one way or another.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so I went over. That was the same person, [INAUDIBLE], the leader of farm. And I said to him, can you use one of my girls there?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, oh, certainly I can. So I made my choice. And she would've stayed-- liked to stay with me. And I thought it would have been better that she went to [INAUDIBLE] because I found her a better match for him, which afterwards, has proven to be right. But at that particular moment, she wasn't keen at all to leave. She would have thought that the other one, Mimi [? Vancouver ?] would have joined [INAUDIBLE]. But that straightened itself out. Afterwards, they were quite agreeable. They thought-- they believed me that I did the right thing, yeah. And quite a number have died. [INAUDIBLE] I've still got here a picture, which I always say, that's my second father. He was a beaut. He was really a beaut. Oh, nice fellow.

speaker: interviewer
text: And today, do you dream about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, quite regularly. As it's-- especially over the last year again where all the remembrances were on. Hopeless. And this night, I haven't slept at all either. Because yesterday, we showed the film again, of the one I-- to someone else. And then everything come back again. The whole thing, the whole situation, what you were doing. And all the time, it's that the fight to, to keep people out of the transport. That was the biggest fight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you do what you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Conviction. Just conviction. I mean, I didn't believe in what the Germans were doing. For me, is a person a person. It doesn't matter how he is. He's a living person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why should we treat them differently than others? Nobody knows how he is born. Nobody can help that. That we are different, OK. But that doesn't mean that we have to fight each other for it. No, not in my book. Have you told your children the details of what you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Our children have not seen any difference, and they know it. We treat everybody the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they--

speaker: interviewee
text: But we never told the stories, no way. Look, the children were small when we had all these things. And why to bother these children with these things afterwards when they came to, to adulthood? It was never a point. That's past. That's history. That history came up back again just because of the, the different sort of interest that the, the world is showing now towards the Jewish people. That doesn't mean that I have to tell what I did for the Jewish people. It's not important.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the first thing that they actually got to know-- yes, they knew-- because all these Jewish friends we had, were for our children, uncles and aunts, but nothing else. And there was never, never a talk about how good we were, or how well we behaved or something like that. There was not a talk. Why should it? So they found out, actually, these things far later. And when we got then medal, that's where they got to know actually what was [? on, ?] because they joined in in the meeting. Yeah. So do you have a message for them? Would you like to say something to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, to behave the same way as I taught them, taught them. That's it. Treat everyone the way you want to be treated. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much. Hang on. Can you tell me about this album?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this is actually our family album. And in the camp, during the war, there was no possibility of having photos made anymore because there were no films available. But in the camp, there was a photographer. And he had to do some jobs for the Germans. For its-- films were made available. And he saved one of these films for us to make these photos. And therefore, we got this professional [? pad ?] of album.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Bresswaler. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And was he Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, German Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me about the illustrations on the page? the illustrations were made also by a Jewish boy. He was [INAUDIBLE]. And his name was Leo [? Koch. ?] He was really a fabulous artist. And well, he didn't survive. He survived the war so far. He was liberated, but died of typhus thereafter. And what happened to [? Bresswaler? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Bresswaler ?] was transported on the 1st of September to Auschwitz and never returned.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do the illustrations mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the illustrations mean-- actually, it represents a bit of the situation, how it was in camp. It was a very windy place, and there was hardly anything around. We had some lakes, small lakes, in which there were some ducks. And that's the reason why he put the ducks up and he showed the wind also, which was blowing practically all day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me what it says underneath the center photograph? And who is the photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's where our second one was born. And of course, we were quite excited about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is--

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's what you find underneath then-- how the, the girls of the family. We had there the whole family together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it say under the center photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: On what? One of the photos?

speaker: interviewer
text: The center photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they're in ecstasy, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Papa in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Papa--

speaker: interviewer
text: --Ecstasy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who is in the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's-- you want all the persons?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the--

speaker: interviewer
text: And their names.

speaker: interviewee
text: The left one is [? Heni. ?] That's the eldest one. Then my wife. Then in the middle, that's [? Nelly. ?] That's the girl who was born there. She was there about a fortnight. And that's me there next to it. The whole family, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, those pictures were made of-- also by [? Bresswaler. ?] But the drawings were done by Leo Koch. Leo Koch was a boy of about 21 when I met him. And he was a very, very good artist. And he made those drawings of me, and of my wife, and of the eldest one. That's why she sits on the potty. And the top picture there, that shows you a drawing of the toilets, how they were, for the bigger barracks in the camp. These were just open pans, as I said, to sit on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me about these pages?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's [? Heni ?] on her own. So there she was still-- that was before the second one was born. And because he found her so photogenic, photogenic, that he wanted to make more pictures of her. And that's the reason why we have gotten all these small pictures of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the illustrations?

speaker: interviewee
text: The illustrations were done by the same. We had rabbits, for instance, in the camp. That's why he put the rabbits on there. We had a dog. And this, because of the tallness of the picture, he invented there for the giraffes to put them in there. Yeah, he was a good, a very, very good artist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he work for other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he did quite a number of pictures, drawings, in the camp. And there is now, in the museum in Westerbork, since-- [CLEARS THROAT] Since that, I think in '81, they came to the conclusion that they had nothing about Westerbork itself in the form of a museum. And that's why they start opening it up. And now they've got quite a collection of these drawings of Leo Koch there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [? Bresswaler, ?] is he also--

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Bresswaler? ?] Oh yes. Quite a number of pictures. I gave quite a lot to them too. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Can you tell me who this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is Lou Cohen. And that's the one whom I always liked to call my second father. He was marvelous for us, for the whole family. And he was always with us when he was in the camp. He actually lived in the camp next door to me. We were neighbors. But he loved our family. He didn't have any children of himself, which he had-- pitied very much. And our eldest one, that was his favorite.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph was taken, I think-- I'm not sure there. But that must have been in beginning of the '50s.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He died of a heart attack in 1968, January, 1968. He was only 67.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did he stay in Westerbork till the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. He was there for-- A year and a half. He went over to Theresienstadt with the last transport in-- on the 3rd of September. And from Theresienstadt, there was an exchange going on at one stage for German soldiers or whatever. And he was exchanged then and transported from Theresienstadt to Switzerland. And people could apply for that changeover. And he said to his family-- he had his wife and his mother with him-- and he said, well, the first thing what I mentioned is the right one. Don't wait for the second. And that's when he said, we decided to go. And so he came to Switzerland. And from Switzerland, he went to the liberated part of France. And he lived for quite some time in Paris. There, he joined the army, and was a captain in the first instance, later became a lieutenant colonel.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as soon as he got to know that we were liberated, I got a letter from him. It was the first thing. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. So we'll just do this one, Harry. And then we'll-- can you tell me about this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That picture was taken when we got the letter from [INAUDIBLE] that we were honored with the Medal for the Righteous. And that was taken by the They put an article in the about the-- when the medal was given to us. That was on the 29th of April, 1992, by the consulate general. [? Gore ?] was his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. Who's that photograph of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture of the boy I told you about, Heinus Eidefeld. He went on his own free will, ultimately, to Germany, which I could-- only, the only thing that they could do for him at that particular time that he didn't go to Auschwitz, but instead to Theresienstadt. Although later on, we found out then that he was redrafted, transported to Auschwitz and didn't survive. But on the transport, when he left the camp, he threw out of the train two cards written to us in which he farewelled us. And the first card says in translation, 'I write you this card in the hope that you will receive it since I'm accustomed to the idea that I'm leaving now. I submit it-- to it. You and your wife will always be an example to me, in perhaps difficult coming days. Now I'm sure of your lasting friendship for me. A lot of things has become easier. Furthermore, I wish to congratulate you, Mrs. van As, with your birthday. I could add a lot more to it, but that is not necessarily. We do understand each other, also without it. Greet the people known to us. I wish you and your family the best of all. I am strong. Regards from Felix. Till we meet again.' And that was signed by him, Heinus. And the second card is as follows there. [CLEARS THROAT] 'My dear family van As. There goes my second card. Again, I hope you will receive it. We just passed [INAUDIBLE]. That was close to the border. I've sent Felix off to bed. He's OK. I reserved the rear cabin of the wagon for the two of us. It's not warm, but we can bear it. Writing is difficult because the carriage is jolting a lot. We are approaching the border. Give everybody our greetings, especially Mr. Cohen and Felix' brother and wife. For the last time, my very best wishes. Till we meet again.' Signed, Heinus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Would you like to introduce your wife to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's my wife, since the beginning. [LAUGHTER] It's still the same one. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: And we hope to do it for another number of years too, huh?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Bertha [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: That's Dutch.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. What do you want me to say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Say something about your husband, about how you worked together.

speaker: interviewee
text: We worked together for all those years, and we are still doing it, just the same way as we did ever before-- perhaps even more intensive than before. We have no secrets from each other. Never has been, and will never be.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's right. We've done everything together. [CLEARS THROAT] Before the war, during the war, and after. So we migrated together to Australia so our children grow up here, get married, have children. And now we are in the stage that grandchildren are getting married. [CLEARS THROAT] We've got three granddaughters who are married. One has got twins, two little girls. So our family is getting bigger and bigger. And we are getting more busy to raise the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: We've got now a photo already for four generations.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So life has been good to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all right.
